document.target_audience: Another agent dedicated to homelab + Linux/OS configuration

high_level_goal.primary_objective: Build a production-like, cloud-native homelab on a single node that demonstrates Linux + DevOps skills through tangible assets: k3s, observability, storage design, backups, and operational discipline.
secondary_objectives: Keep the node headless and operator-driven (SSH + web UIs) to mirror real-world environments.
Create a clean separation of concerns across storage tiers (OS, platform/observability, databases, backups).
Enable future expansion to multi-node and hybrid homelab-cloud patterns without re-architecting.

intended_workloads: Observability stack (Prometheus, Loki, Grafana, Alertmanager, exporters)
workload_profile.access_pattern: Admin via SSH; dashboards and UIs via browser from a separate dev machine (Mac).

machine.positioning: Headless 'private cloud node' rather than a desktop workstation

nvme_boot_constraint.status: Assumed: NVMe not used as boot device in this plan
reasoning: Older workstation/BIOS scenarios can have unreliable or unsupported NVMe boot paths depending on firmware and configuration.
Even if NVMe boot is possible, isolating boot/OS on SATA SSD simplifies recovery and reduces blast radius.
nvme_boot_constraint.impact: Boot remains stable and simple; NVMe drives are reserved for high-performance tiers.

cooling.nvme_adapters: PCIe M.2 adapters with heatsinks are preferred for sustained writes (metrics/logging/DB WAL).
cooling.impact: Lower risk of thermal throttling; better long-run stability.

reasoning: Observability stack is web-based; no GUI required on server.
Desktop environment adds background services, increases noise in metrics, and consumes RAM/CPU.
Server aligns with real production operations: SSH, systemd, journald, logrotate, CLI-first workflows.
positive_impact: Cleaner telemetry signal (metrics reflect workloads, not GNOME/desktop activity).
Lower resource overhead (more capacity for Prometheus/Loki/DB).
More realistic DevOps story for interviews (operator mindset).

access_model.dashboards: Browser access (Grafana, etc.) from dev machine

layers.purpose: Stable boot + OS + core system services
layers.principle: Minimize churn; keep clean and recoverable
High IOPS + high write endurance usage belongs on NVMe

layers.purpose: Postgres + Redis + WAL on fast dedicated media
layers.principle: Avoid contention; preserve predictable DB performance

layers.purpose: Backups, snapshots exports, long retention logs/archives

how_this_design_helps: Clear separation of OS/platform/data makes it easier to migrate tiers to another node.
Backups on HDD can seed restore to a new machine.
k3s and GitOps can rehydrate cluster state on new hardware.
future_scaling_intent.note: Adding a second node implies another physical machine (or a VM host) joining the k3s cluster.

storage_plan.summary: Each drive has a specific operational responsibility to reduce contention, simplify recovery, and tell a strong DevOps story.

drive_roles_and_justification.role: Boot drive + OS + k3s binaries (system drive)
what_lives_here: Minimal logs (journald/logrotate default) but not heavy application logs
why: Stable boot path and straightforward recovery.
Keeps OS isolated from high-write application and telemetry data.
Reduces risk that runaway metrics/logs fill up the OS disk.
positive_impact: Faster incident recovery: OS disk remains predictable.
Cleaner upgrades/patching and easier rollback strategies.
Lower risk of cluster downtime caused by OS disk exhaustion.

drive_roles_and_justification.role: Platform tier: k3s runtime storage + observability persistence
what_lives_here: Container runtime data directory (e.g., /var/lib/rancher/k3s or equivalent path relocation)
Persistent volumes for observability components (Prometheus TSDB, Loki chunks/index, Grafana storage as needed)
why: Metrics/logging is write-heavy; NVMe handles high IOPS and sustained writes better than SATA/HDD.
Separates platform churn from OS stability and DB performance.
positive_impact: More reliable Prometheus/Loki performance (fewer ingestion bottlenecks).
Improved platform resilience and easier capacity management.
Cleaner troubleshooting: platform disk alerts are isolated and actionable.

what_lives_here: Postgres WAL (either colocated or intentionally placed here to avoid OS/platform disks)
Any other stateful app data requiring low latency
why: DB + WAL are latency-sensitive and write-intensive; dedicated NVMe reduces tail latency and contention.
Separating DB I/O from observability ingestion avoids performance cliffs.
positive_impact: Predictable query performance and faster recovery/repairs.
Reduced risk of noisy-neighbor effects from logs/metrics pipelines.
Stronger 'realistic platform ops' story (data tier treated seriously).

what_lives_here: Database backups (logical + physical strategies depending on tooling)
Compressed archives of logs/artifacts if needed
Optional local 'cold storage' for large files/datasets
why: Backups are capacity-oriented and sequential; HDD is cost-effective for retention.
Separates backups from failure modes of the hot tiers (NVMe wear, file system corruption, etc.).
positive_impact: Improved resilience: local restore path even if NVMe tiers fail.
Supports operational maturity: backup rotation and retention policies.
Clear auditability: backup artifacts stored in a designated tier.

key_operational_principles: Avoid mixing OS and high-churn workloads on the same disk.
Avoid mixing observability ingestion (write-heavy) with DB WAL (also write-heavy).
Keep backups on a separate physical medium to reduce correlated failures.
Prefer mounts by UUID, explicit filesystem options, and monitoring of disk usage.

reasoning: Lightweight, quick to bootstrap, excellent for single-node lab and later multi-node expansion.
Good alignment with cloud-native DevOps skills (ingress, cert-manager, PVCs, monitoring).

observability.goal: Measure, alert, and debug failures like in real production

why_this_matters: Matches real DevOps environment operations (no GUI on servers).
Improves signal quality of observability and reduces resource overhead.

primary_risks: Disk fill on OS drive causing instability
Thermal throttling on NVMe under sustained writes
Write amplification / wear on smaller NVMe used for observability
Backup strategy not tested (false sense of security)
mitigations: Heatsinks on NVMe adapters + airflow awareness
Disk monitoring + alerts (Prometheus node exporter, alert rules)
Routine restore tests (simulate DB restore monthly)

technical_outcomes: Ubuntu Server boots reliably from SATA SSD
k3s runs cleanly with persistent storage mapped to the correct tiers
Prometheus + Loki ingest and retain data without exhausting disks
Postgres + Redis stable performance and clean backup/restore procedure
Backups stored on HDD with retention and periodic restore validation
career_outcomes: Clear narrative: designed and operated a production-like platform (storage tiers, k8s, observability, backups).
Demonstrable artifacts: diagrams, IaC, runbooks, alert rules, dashboards, incident simulations.

what_to_focus_on_next: Ubuntu Server installation plan (partitioning + mounts by UUID).
Filesystem choices and mount options for each tier.
Relocating k3s/container runtime directories to the platform NVMe.
PersistentVolume strategy aligned to physical disks.
Backup tooling selection and test restore procedure.
Baseline security hardening (SSH keys, firewall, fail2ban optional).
guardrails: Keep server headless; do not install desktop environment.
Preserve tier boundaries; do not co-mingle DB and observability on the same disk if avoidable.
Monitor disk usage early and set alerts before the system is 'full'.
